mnemonics.title: VAR Rule
mnemonics.content: VAR = Variable with Automatic Type inference, but Remember: Only for Local variables, Always initialize, Restricted contexts only.

mnemonics.title: L-A-V-E-R Memory Aid
mnemonics.content: L - Local only, A - Always initialize, V - Void methods okay, E - Eliminates verbosity, R - Runtime safe (compile-time inference).

acronyms.expansion: Java Enhancement Proposal - JEP 286 introduced var in Java 10
Java Language Specification - defines var behavior
acronyms.usage: JLS Section 14.4 defines local variable type inference rules

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flow: Right-hand side expression → Compiler analyzes type → Compiler assigns inferred type → Type stored in bytecode → No runtime overhead
Side-by-side boxes: var list = new ArrayList<String>() → Box labeled 'ArrayList<String>'; Object list = new ArrayList<String>() → Box labeled 'Object' (loses type info)

visualTips.visualize: Circle diagram showing: Local variables (INSIDE) ✓, Method parameters (OUTSIDE) ✗, Instance variables (OUTSIDE) ✗, Return types (OUTSIDE) ✗

analogies.analogy: Just as a phone predicts your next word based on context, the Java compiler 'predicts' or infers the type based on the value you assign - but unlike phone predictions, it's always correct because it's verified at compile time.

Think of var as a blank on a form that gets filled in by the compiler. You write 'var' and the compiler fills in the actual type, like a teacher grading a test - they know exactly what type was intended.
Like a pair of glasses with one lens missing (the type), var fills in Object as the default - you can see, but not clearly. Always specify types when possible.
